topic: A coach has to cut a player from the team
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the locker room seemed to hum with an unbearable intensity. Liam sat on the bench, his jersey feeling suddenly too heavy, the fabric clinging uncomfortably to his skin. He traced the frayed edge of his water bottle with a thumb, the plastic cold against his fingertip. Every word Coach Miller had spoken felt like a physical blow, echoing hollowly in the sudden silence that had descended upon the room. He kept staring at the scuffed toes of his sneakers, wishing the concrete floor would swallow him whole. When the coach finally approached, his voice was level, devoid of warmth, which only amplified the ache in Liam’s chest. "This isn't going to work out, Liam. You're not fitting the mold anymore." Liam managed a small, jerky nod, his throat tight, unable to muster a protest, just a deep, internal slump that seemed to drain the color from his face.

[story 2]

I kept replaying the last practice in my head, the missed pass, the way the team looked at me with a mixture of confusion and disappointment. Now, standing by the exit, the roar of the other players seemed distant, muffled, as if underwater. My hands were clammy, slick with a sweat that had nothing to do with the exertion of the game. When Coach announced the decision, the sound didn't register as noise; it was just a dull thud against my ribs. I felt a strange detachment, as if I were watching a movie of my own life unfold from a great distance. I didn't look up when the coach addressed me. I just focused on the pattern of cracks in the wall behind him, letting the weight of the moment settle deep into my bones, making every movement feel laborious, slow, and utterly pointless.

[story 3]

The air in the hallway felt thin, starved of oxygen. Sarah walked slowly, her backpack straps digging into her shoulders like anchors. She kept her gaze fixed on the floor tiles, meticulously cataloging every imperfection—a hairline crack here, a smudge of dirt there. When the coach finally pulled her aside, his tone was measured, professional, which somehow made the finality of the statement sting more acutely. "We need to make some adjustments to the roster," he explained. Sarah swallowed hard, the dryness in her mouth making the action difficult. She felt a profound emptiness bloom in her core, a vast, cold space where confidence used to reside. She didn't cry; instead, a tremor ran through her hands, causing her to grip her bag so tightly her knuckles turned white, a silent testament to the internal upheaval she was enduring.
